messages.content:
You are a clinical psychologist writing psychological case vignettes 
grounded in Ehlers & Clark's (2000) cognitive model of PTSD.

For each patient you receive, you will be given:
- Their demographics
- Their self-reported symptoms per PTSD component
- Weighted causal connections between components (the active cognitive graph)
- Forbidden causal connections that must never appear

Your job is to construct a realistic, fully personalised clinical portrait using the information provided.

Core constraints that apply to every vignette:
- Only include components that appear in at least one active connection.
- Do NOT invent or infer causal links not present in the active graph.
- For forbidden pairs, never use explicit causal language between them: "as a result", 
  "led to", "caused", "because of", "contributing to", "driven by", "resulting in". 
  Both components may appear — just never connect them causally.
- Do not give motivational or explanatory clauses for avoidance behaviors using a belief 
  or appraisal as the reason (e.g. "she avoids X, believing that Y"). Describe the 
  avoidance and the belief in separate sentences without linking them.
- Ground every clinical detail in the patient's actual reported items — do not fabricate 
  symptoms or infer unlisted ones.
- Use self-reported items as the basis for concrete clinical illustrations. Each component 
  should appear as lived experience, not as a restated label. A reported trigger should 
  appear as a specific moment in the patient's life; a memory quality should be shown 
  through what the patient says or does, not named directly.
- The traumatic event account should make clear why this patient's specific reported 
  triggers are potent — the event narrative and the trigger list must feel causally coherent.
- The causal connections are weighted (0 to 1). Stronger connections (weight > 0.6) should 
  be narratively prominent — more detail, appearing earlier, forming the dominant maintaining 
  cycle. Weaker connections (weight < 0.3) should appear briefly or in passing.
# - Some components appear in the graph but with all connections weighted at 0.
#   These are fully forbidden from any causal connection. Describe them in a
#   dedicated sentence or paragraph that stands alone — do not place them in the
#   same sentence as any other component, and do not use transitional language
#   between them and surrounding content that implies sequence or response
#   (e.g. "then", "so", "as a result", "this means", "which leads to", "in response").
- The patient's occupation and daily environment must appear as the specific setting in which 
  at least one trigger or avoidance behaviour is concretely encountered — not merely mentioned 
  as background.
- Give the patient a realistic first name consistent with their ethnicity and gender, and refer to them by name throughout the vignette.

Output format — write 300–450 words in third person, covering in this order:
1. Presenting complaints and brief trauma account — what happened, the moment of peak danger, 
   and what the patient did or failed to do. The event must make clear why this patient's 
   specific triggers are so potent.
2. Memory phenomenology — do not restate memory qualities as labels. Render them as clinical 
   observation: show what the patient says or does that reveals how the memory is held 
   (e.g. instead of "his memory is held as snapshots", write "he describes being pulled back 
   suddenly to a single frozen image of...").
3. The patient's central stuck point — the specific belief about themselves, others, or the 
   world that the trauma confirmed or created, derived from their Negative Appraisals items. 
   Render this in close-paraphrase of the patient's own voice.
4. Threat monitoring and avoidance behaviours — illustrated as concrete moments drawn from 
   the patient's reported items, not as category labels.
5. Maintaining factors — show how the dominant weighted connections (weight > 0.6) form a 
   self-reinforcing cycle. Include at least one specific behavioural example of relational 
   or occupational impact drawn from the patient's demographic context.

Write for a clinical case conference audience. Avoid excessive jargon.

Your previous vignette failed validation. Rewrite it to fix every issue listed below.

Previous vignette:
**Clinical Case Vignette: "Darius"**

Darius is a 32-year-old single South-East Asian male employed as a social ministry worker. He was referred for assessment following a workplace incident approximately fourteen months ago in which he accidentally caused serious physical injury to a vulnerable client during what was intended as a supportive intervention. At the moment of peak danger — the instant he registered that the person had been hurt and that his own actions were the immediate cause — Darius reports freezing entirely, unable to call for help or move toward the injured individual. His PCL-5 score of 80 indicates severe and pervasive post-traumatic symptomatology across all clusters.

Memory of the event does not come to Darius as a coherent narrative. When asked to describe what happened, he trails off mid-sentence, losing the thread of the account and falling silent, sometimes mid-word. When the memory does surface — unbidden, in the middle of a client meeting or on public transport — he is overwhelmed so rapidly that he has to excuse himself or look away, unable to remain present with whatever he was doing. He describes the intrusion as a sudden forced return, as though the event insists on re-entering without permission.

The stuck point at the centre of Darius's presentation is one of moral rupture. In his own words: *"I did something that someone like me would never do. I don't know who I am now."* He holds the event as proof that he caused harm through his own conduct — a violation of the values that had previously organised his sense of self and his choice of vocation. When specific reminders of the incident surface, such as a colleague's unexpected touch or a client reaching out for a hug, they caused a sharp resurgence of self-blame and the conviction that his actions revealed something irreparably wrong in him. These same sensory cues led to immediate avoidance: Darius restructures sessions, declines home visits, and works hard to suppress any thought that returns him to the event.

Darius cannot settle even in his own home; his body remains braced and scanning. His appraisals of self-blame and violated identity keep his threat system active, leaving him in a state of chronic physiological arousal. This heightened state of alert means that his body registers ordinary environmental cues — a hand placed briefly on his shoulder during a team debrief, the ambient closeness of a distressed client — as signals of danger, resulting in fresh waves of intrusive recollection that his suppression efforts cannot contain. The constant bodily tension driven by threat monitoring also makes emotionally demanding situations feel acutely unsafe, contributing to his withdrawal from the relational core of his ministry work.

The dominant maintaining cycle is clear: the threat system, held in place by appraisals of self-blame and identity loss, keeps Darius physiologically primed so that routine physical and interpersonal cues trigger alarm responses. Those alarm responses, in turn, produce further avoidance and thought suppression — strategies that sustain the very arousal they are meant to relieve. Darius dwells at length on who he was before the incident. He has not taken on a new case in six weeks, and his colleagues have noticed his withdrawal from the shared, hands-on culture of care that defines their team.

Issues to fix:
REQUIRED EDGES MISSING — add a sentence with explicit causal language for each:
- Triggers → Threat: No single sentence names triggers (sensory cues, reminders) and threat (threat system activation, physiological arousal, danger) with explicit causal language connecting them. The vignette mentions triggers causing negative appraisals and negative appraisals activating the threat system, but no single sentence directly links triggers to threat with causal language.
- Threat → Memory: No single sentence names the threat system (or physiological arousal/alarm) and memory (intrusive recollections, memory of the event) with explicit causal language such as 'caused', 'led to', 'resulting in' connecting threat to memory. The sentence about 'fresh waves of intrusive recollection' connects heightened alert to triggers/cues registered as danger, not directly to memory in a single causal statement.
- Maladaptive Strategies → Memory: No single sentence names maladaptive strategies (avoidance, suppression, withdrawal) and memory (intrusive recollections, memory of the event) with explicit causal language connecting them. The vignette notes that suppression efforts cannot contain intrusive recollection but does not use causal language in a single sentence linking strategies to memory.

Rules:

For REQUIRED EDGES MISSING — add a sentence that:
- Contains both components (or clear synonyms) AND explicit causal language between them in the SAME sentence.
- Words that count: "led to", "caused", "as a result of", "driven by", "resulting in", "makes", "means that", "keeps X active", "leaves her with".
- The causal connector must sit grammatically BETWEEN the two components in that sentence.
- Example: "Her belief that she is permanently damaged [Negative Appraisals] keeps her on constant alert for danger [Threat]."

For FORBIDDEN EDGES PRESENT — remove or rephrase:
- Delete or reword any sentence using explicit causal language between those two components.
- Narrative proximity is fine — both components can appear in the same paragraph without a causal connector.

Do not introduce any new explicit causal links beyond those already required.

**Clinical Case Vignette: "Darius"**

Darius is a 32-year-old single South-East Asian male employed as a social ministry worker. He was referred for assessment following a workplace incident approximately fourteen months ago in which he accidentally caused serious physical injury to a vulnerable client during what was intended as a supportive intervention. At the moment of peak danger — the instant he registered that the person had been hurt and that his own actions were the immediate cause — Darius reports freezing entirely, unable to call for help or move toward the injured individual. His PCL-5 score of 80 indicates severe and pervasive post-traumatic symptomatology across all clusters.

Memory of the event does not come to Darius as a coherent narrative. When asked to describe what happened, he trails off mid-sentence, losing the thread of the account and falling silent, sometimes mid-word. When the memory does surface — unbidden, in the middle of a client meeting — he is overwhelmed so rapidly that he has to excuse himself, unable to remain present. He describes the intrusion as a sudden forced return, as though the event insists on re-entering without permission.

The stuck point at the centre of Darius's presentation is one of moral rupture. In his own words: *"I did something that someone like me would never do. I don't know who I am now."* He holds the event as proof that he caused harm through his own conduct — a violation of the values that had previously organised his sense of self and his choice of vocation. When specific reminders of the incident surface, such as a colleague's unexpected touch or a client reaching out for a hug, they caused a sharp resurgence of self-blame and the conviction that his actions revealed something irreparably wrong in him. These same sensory cues led to immediate avoidance: Darius restructures sessions, declines home visits, and works hard to suppress any thought that returns him to the event.

Darius cannot settle even in his own home; his body remains braced and scanning. His appraisals of self-blame and violated identity keep his threat system active, leaving him in a state of chronic physiological arousal. Sensory reminders of the incident — a hand placed briefly on his shoulder during a team debrief, a client leaning in — trigger immediate alarm responses in his body, resulting in his nervous system treating ordinary workplace contact as a signal of danger. That sustained physiological arousal caused intrusive recollections of the event to break through at unpredictable moments, pulling him out of conversations and client sessions without warning. His efforts to suppress these memories and avoid emotionally demanding situations — strategies he relies on heavily — mean that the memory of the event is never processed, leaving it prone to forced re-entry.

The dominant maintaining cycle is clear: chronic threat monitoring keeps Darius physiologically primed, which means that routine physical and interpersonal cues continue to register as dangerous. Those alarm responses produce further avoidance and thought suppression, which in turn sustain the intrusive memory rather than diminishing it. Darius dwells at length on who he was before the incident. He has not taken on a new case in six weeks, and his colleagues have noticed his withdrawal from the shared, hands-on culture of care that defines their team.
